modules.title: Temperature and thermometers
modules.html:
Temperature is a measure of the degree of hotness or coldness of a body. Scales: Celsius (°C), Kelvin (K).

Conversion: T(K) = θ(°C) + 273.
Fahrenheit: F = 9/5·C + 32.

Types of thermometers

  • Liquid-in-glass: mercury (−39 to 357 °C) or alcohol (−115 to 78 °C).
  • Gas thermometer: uses constant-volume gas; wide range, very accurate.
  • Resistance (platinum) thermometer: R depends on T; suits large ranges.
  • Thermoelectric (thermocouple): EMF vs T for two metals; measures rapid changes.

modules.title: Expansivity
modules.html:
Linear expansion: ΔL = αLΔθ.
Area expansion: ΔA = βAΔθ, β ≈ 2α.
Volume (cubic) expansion: ΔV = γVΔθ, γ ≈ 3α.

Liquids expand more than solids; gases expand the most.

Anomalous expansion of water: from 0 to 4 °C water contracts; above 4 °C it expands. Maximum density at 4 °C.

examples.q: A 1 m rod, α=12×10⁻⁶/K. Expansion for ΔT=50 K?
Why is water at bottom of a pond in winter always ≈4 °C?
examples.a: Water is densest at 4 °C, so denser water sinks.

modules.title: Gas laws
modules.html:
Boyle's law: at constant T, PV = constant.
Charles's law: at constant P, V/T = constant.
Pressure (Gay-Lussac) law: at constant V, P/T = constant.
General gas law: P₁V₁/T₁ = P₂V₂/T₂.

Ideal gas equation: PV = nRT (R = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹).

examples.q: Gas at 27°C, 1 atm and 300 mL heated to 127°C at same P. New volume?
Compress a gas from 400 mL to 200 mL at constant T; original pressure 100 kPa. New pressure?

modules.title: Specific heat and latent heat
modules.html:
Specific heat capacity c = Q/(mΔT). Water: 4200 J·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹.

Method of mixtures: heat lost by hot body = heat gained by cold body (assuming no losses).

Latent heat: heat absorbed during change of state without change of temperature.
L_f (fusion) for ice: 3.36×10⁵ J/kg.
L_v (vaporisation) for water: 2.26×10⁶ J/kg.

Q = ml.

examples.q: Heat to warm 2 kg water from 20 to 80 °C (c=4200):

modules.title: Evaporation, humidity, heat transfer
modules.html:
Evaporation happens at any temperature, only at the surface. Boiling occurs at a specific temperature, throughout the liquid.

Relative humidity = mass of water vapour in air / mass required for saturation × 100%. Measured with a hygrometer.

Heat transfer

  • Conduction: through solids by particle vibration and (in metals) free electrons.
  • Convection: in fluids by movement of warm particles.
  • Radiation: by electromagnetic waves, needs no medium.

Vacuum flasks reduce all three modes: silvered walls (radiation), vacuum (conduction/convection), tight cork (conduction/convection).

examples.q: Give the mode of heat loss from the Sun to the Earth.
Why does sweating cool the body?
examples.a: Evaporation absorbs latent heat from the skin.
